title: Apple Snow
original_recipe:
NO. 27.--APPLE SNOW.

      1. Pare and slice apples, dropping into cold water.

      2. Cook slowly until soft.

      3. Mash well and measure.

      4. To every cupful, allow

    1/4 cup powdered sugar,
    white of 1 egg, well beaten.

      5. Add a spoonful of each to apples until used,
      stirring in lightly each time.

      6. Add 1 drop vanilla for each cup of apples.

      7. Serve with cream.
modern_ingredients: 4 medium apples (tart varieties like Granny Smith work best), peeled and sliced
For every cup of cooked apple purée: 1/4 cup powdered sugar and 1 egg white, beaten stiff
1 drop vanilla extract per cup of purée
modern_instructions: Peel, core, and slice apples. Drop into cold water as you work to prevent browning.
Drain and cook apples in a saucepan over low heat with just a splash of water, stirring occasionally, until completely soft, about 15–20 minutes.
Mash apples until smooth. Measure the purée.
Beat the egg whites to stiff peaks. Do not add sugar until stiff.
Gently fold alternating spoonfuls of powdered sugar and beaten egg whites into the warm apple purée, one spoonful at a time, until all are incorporated.
Add one drop of vanilla per cup of purée and fold in gently.
Spoon into individual glasses or a serving bowl. Chill briefly.
Serve with a pour of cold heavy cream.

title: Baked Apples
original_recipe:
NO. 15.--BAKED APPLES.

      1. Wipe large apples.

      2. Take out cores with apple corer or sharp knife.

      3. Place in earthen or enamel dish.

      4. Fill center of each apple with sugar.

      5. Pour water into dish, allowing 2 tablespoons to
      each apple.

      6. Bake in a hot oven one half hour, or until soft.
modern_ingredients: 4 large baking apples (Granny Smith, Honeycrisp, or Rome)
Optional: cinnamon, raisins, butter, or maple syrup to fill
modern_instructions: Preheat oven to 375°F (190°C).
Wipe apples clean. Core each apple from the top, leaving the bottom intact (use an apple corer or paring knife), creating a cavity about 3/4 inch wide.
Place apples upright in a baking dish.
Fill each cavity with 1 tablespoon of sugar (or a mix of brown sugar and cinnamon).
Add 2 tablespoons of water around each apple.
Bake 30–40 minutes until the apples are completely soft when pierced with a fork and the skins are starting to burst.
Serve warm with cream, ice cream, or custard.

title: Baked Potatoes
original_recipe:
NO. 5.--BAKED POTATOES.

      1. Choose potatoes of the same size--rather large.

      2. Scrub well, and wipe.

      3. Bake in a hot oven from 30 to 45 minutes, or until
      soft when pressed between thumb and fingers.

      4. Roll each between the fingers: this makes them
      mealy.

      5. Serve on a napkin.
modern_ingredients: 4 large russet potatoes, uniform in size
Optional: butter, sour cream, salt for serving
modern_instructions: Preheat oven to 425°F (220°C).
Scrub potatoes thoroughly and pat dry.
Prick each potato several times with a fork (modern practice to prevent bursting).
Place directly on the oven rack or on a baking sheet.
Bake 45–60 minutes, until very soft when squeezed gently with a towel-protected hand.
Remove from oven and roll each potato back and forth briefly between your palms — this helps create a fluffy, mealy interior.
Serve on a folded napkin or cloth.

title: Baking Powder Biscuits
original_recipe:
NO. 17.--BAKING POWDER BISCUITS.

      1. Make exactly the same as Thimble Biscuits (No. 16),
      only pat the dough out about three-quarters of an inch
      thick. Prick with a fork.

      2. Cut with a biscuit cutter, and place in pan, a
      little distance apart.

      3. Bake in a quick oven from 12 to 14 minutes.
modern_instructions: Preheat oven to 450°F (230°C). Grease a baking sheet.
Sift together flour, baking powder, and salt three times.
Rub in the cold butter with fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs.
Add milk gradually, mixing with a knife just until a soft dough forms.
Turn onto a floured surface and pat or roll to 3/4 inch thickness.
Prick the surface with a fork.
Cut with a 2-inch round biscuit cutter. Place on prepared pan a little distance apart.
Bake 12–14 minutes until risen and golden on top.

title: Boiled Mutton
original_recipe:
NO. 25.--BOILED MUTTON.

      About 3 pounds rack of mutton or "yearling."

      1. Wipe with a damp cloth.

      2. Pour 3 cups boiling water into a large pot.

      3. Throw in 2 peeled onions.

      4. Put in the meat. Cover.

      5. Boil 10 minutes.

      6. Draw pot to back of stove where it will simmer, or
      just bubble, until meat is tender when tried with a
      fork, which will be in about 1-1/2 hours.

      7. Take out the meat.

      8. Skim off the fat from the surface of the liquor; or
      if there is time, cool, and remove the hardened fat.
modern_ingredients: 3 pounds bone-in lamb shoulder, rack of lamb, or lamb chops (mutton is difficult to find today
young lamb is the best substitute)
modern_instructions: Wipe the meat with a damp cloth.
Bring 3 cups of water to a boil in a large heavy pot. Add the peeled onions.
Add the meat to the pot. Cover.
Boil hard for 10 minutes.
Reduce heat to a very gentle simmer (barely bubbling). Cover and cook 1.5 hours, or until the meat is very tender when pierced with a fork.
Remove the meat from the pot and keep warm.
Skim fat from the surface of the cooking liquid, or refrigerate the liquid to solidify and remove the fat layer before reheating.
Use the defatted liquid to make Parsley Gravy (No. 26).

title: Boiled Potatoes
original_recipe:
NO. 6.--BOILED POTATOES.

      1. Wash potatoes.

      2. Pare, throwing into cold water.

      3. Drain; cover with boiling water, allowing 1
      tablespoon salt to every twelve potatoes.

      4. Let boil 1/2 hour, or until the largest is soft
      when pierced with a fork.

      5. Drain off all the water.

      6. Shake over fire, or place on back of stove to dry
      off the potatoes.
modern_instructions: Wash and peel potatoes. Drop into cold water as you peel to prevent browning.
Drain and place in a saucepan. Cover with cold or boiling water and add the salt.
Bring to a boil, then cook at a steady boil for 25–30 minutes, until the largest potato is easily pierced with a fork.
Drain completely.
Return the pot to low heat briefly and shake gently, or leave uncovered in a warm spot, to steam off excess moisture.
Serve plain or use in other recipes.

title: Boiled Rice
original_recipe:
NO. 22.--BOILED RICE.

      Use a plain boiler and a large quantity of salted
      boiling water.

    1/2 cup rice
    5 cups boiling water
    1/2 teaspoon salt

      1. Wash rice by putting it in a strainer and shaking
      in a pan of water.

      2. Drop a few grains at a time into boiling water,
      stirring well to prevent sticking.

      3. Boil rapidly 25 minutes, or until soft when pressed
      between the fingers.

      4. Drain through a colander. Pour over it 2 cups
      boiling water.

      5. Set in a warm place to dry off, until ready to
      serve.
modern_instructions: Rinse rice in a strainer under cold water until the water runs clear.
Bring 5 cups of water to a rolling boil in a large saucepan. Add salt.
Add rice a little at a time, stirring well after each addition.
Boil rapidly, uncovered, for 20–25 minutes until grains are completely soft when pressed between fingers.
Drain through a colander. Pour 2 cups of fresh boiling water over the rice in the colander to rinse.
Return to the warm pot or spread on a warm dish to dry off before serving.

title: Bread Pudding
original_recipe:
NO. 37.--BREAD PUDDING.

      1. Cut slices of stale bread into squares of about one
      half inch.

      2. For 2 cups bread, allow

    1 pint milk (2 cups)
    2 tablespoons sugar
    2 eggs
    1/4 teaspoon vanilla

      3. Moisten bread with hot water.

      4. Butter pudding dish.

      5. Put into it the moistened bread.

      6. Beat yolks of eggs; add sugar; add milk.

      7. Pour this over the bread.

      8. Beat whites of eggs. Add 2 tablespoons powdered
      sugar. Beat well.

      9. Spread this over top of pudding.

      10. Bake in moderate oven one half hour.

      11. Serve with Hard Sauce or cream.
modern_ingredients: 2 cups stale bread, cut into 1/2-inch cubes
modern_instructions: Preheat oven to 350°F (175°C). Butter a 1.5-quart baking dish.
Cut stale bread into 1/2-inch squares.
Pour a small amount of hot water over the bread cubes to moisten them slightly. Drain any excess.
Arrange the moistened bread in the buttered dish.
Beat egg yolks with granulated sugar. Add milk and vanilla, mixing well.
Pour the custard mixture over the bread.
Beat egg whites to soft peaks. Add powdered sugar and beat to stiff, glossy peaks.
Spread the meringue over the top of the pudding.
Bake 30 minutes until the custard is set and the meringue is golden.
Serve warm with Hard Sauce (No. 39) or cream.

title: Cocoa
original_recipe:
NO. 18.--COCOA.

      For each cup:

    1 tablespoon cocoa
    1 tablespoon sugar
    1/2 cup boiling water
    1/2 cup milk

      1. Heat milk.

      2. Mix cocoa and sugar.

      3. Pour boiling water on them gradually, making a
      smooth paste.

      4. Add the milk, and cook a half minute.

      5. Beat with an egg beater.
modern_instructions: Heat the milk in a small saucepan until steaming. Do not boil.
In a mug or small bowl, mix together the cocoa powder and sugar.
Gradually pour the boiling water over the cocoa-sugar mixture, stirring constantly to make a smooth paste, then a liquid.
Add the warm milk and stir well.
Pour into a small saucepan and heat over medium heat for about 30 seconds.
Beat briefly with a small whisk or hand frother until slightly frothy. Serve hot.

title: Coffee
original_recipe:
NO. 11.--COFFEE.

      1. Put into coffee pot 1 rounded tablespoon ground
      coffee for each cup needed.

      2. Pour on boiling water, allowing 1 cup to every
      tablespoon coffee used.

      3. Let come to a boil three times, stirring down each
      time.

      4. Draw off the fire. Pour in 1 tablespoon cold water
      for each cup.

      5. Let stand in a warm place 3 minutes to settle
      grounds. Serve.

      If not used immediately, strain into another warmed
      pot.
modern_instructions: Place ground coffee in a stovetop coffee pot or medium saucepan.
Pour 4 cups of cold water over the grounds and bring to a boil over medium heat.
Each time it comes to a boil, stir it down and let it come to a boil again — repeat 3 times.
Remove from heat. Add 4 tablespoons cold water (1 per cup) to help settle the grounds to the bottom.
Let stand in a warm place for 3 minutes.
Pour slowly through a fine mesh strainer into warmed cups or a pre-warmed pot.

title: Creamed New Cabbage
original_recipe:
NO. 36.--CREAMED NEW CABBAGE.

      1. Cut in half and wash well a young green cabbage.

      2. Cut out and throw away the hard stem part.

      3. Make ready a kettle of boiling water. Put in
      cabbage. Leave uncovered.

      4. When the water boils, throw in 1 teaspoon salt and
      1/4 teaspoon baking soda (or bicarbonate of soda).

      5. Boil gently about half hour, or until it begins to
      lose its bright green color. Lift out with skimmer.

      6. Pour over it White Sauce just before serving.
modern_ingredients: 1 small young green cabbage (or half a large head)
modern_instructions: Cut cabbage in half. Wash well under running water. Remove and discard the hard core.
Bring a large pot of water to a rolling boil. Add the cabbage, uncovered.
When the water returns to a boil, add salt and baking soda.
Boil gently, uncovered, for 20–30 minutes until the cabbage is tender and has begun to lose its bright green color.

title: Dream Cake
original_recipe:
NO. 32.--DREAM CAKE.

    4 tablespoons butter
    1 cup sugar
    2 eggs
    1/2 cup milk
    1-1/4 cups flour
    2 teaspoons baking powder
    1/8 teaspoon ground mace
    dash of salt
    1 teaspoon vanilla

      1. Put butter in a bowl; add sugar. "Cream" or rub
      thoroughly together.

      2. Beat yolks of eggs. Add to butter and sugar,
      stirring well.

      3. Mix and sift twice flour, baking powder, mace and
      salt.

      4. Add one-third of the milk to the yolks, butter and
      sugar.

      5. Sift in one-third of the flour. Do this until all
      the milk and flour are used, beating well each time.

      6. Add vanilla. Beat well.

      7. Beat white of eggs light. Fold them into the cake.

      8. Grease shallow pan as in No. 31. Pour in the cake.

      9. Bake in moderate oven about 35 minutes, or until it
      shrinks from the pan.
modern_instructions: Preheat oven to 350°F (175°C). Grease and flour a 9-inch round pan.
Cream the softened butter and sugar together until light and fluffy, 3–5 minutes.
Beat egg yolks and add to butter-sugar mixture, stirring well.
Sift together flour, baking powder, mace, and salt twice.
Add the milk and flour mixture to the batter alternately in three additions each, beginning and ending with flour. Beat well after each addition.
Add vanilla and beat well.
In a separate bowl, beat egg whites to stiff peaks. Gently fold into the batter.
Pour into prepared pan and smooth the top.
Bake 35 minutes or until the cake shrinks from the sides and a toothpick comes out clean.
Cool in pan 10 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack.

title: Gingerbread Cookies
original_recipe:
NO. 19.--GINGERBREAD COOKIES.

    1/2 cup molasses
    2 tablespoons butter
    2 tablespoons lard flour
    1/2 tablespoon ginger
    1/2 teaspoon salt
    1/2 teaspoon baking soda
    1 tablespoon warm water

      1. Warm the molasses.

      2. Put the butter and lard in a bowl. Pour over them
      the molasses.

      3. Dissolve soda in the warm water. Add to the
      molasses.

      4. Sift ginger, salt and 1/2 cup flour together.

      5. Sift into the molasses, beating well. If necessary,
      add more flour to make a soft dough.

      6. Grease a shallow pan.

      7. Roll the dough out 1/3 inch thick.

      8. Cut out with a little round cutter, dipping it into
      flour each time.

      Place cookies some distance apart on the pan. Bake
      about 10 minutes.

      NOTE.--Instead of rolling out, little spoonfuls may be
      dropped far apart on the pan and flattened with the
      bottom of a round tin cup.
modern_ingredients: 1/2 teaspoon baking soda dissolved in 1 tablespoon warm water
1/2 to 1 cup all-purpose flour (enough to make a soft dough)
modern_instructions: Preheat oven to 375°F (190°C). Grease a baking sheet.
Warm the molasses gently in a small saucepan.
Put butter and shortening in a bowl. Pour the warm molasses over them and stir to melt.
Dissolve baking soda in warm water and add to the molasses mixture.
Sift together ginger, salt, and 1/2 cup flour. Sift into the molasses, beating well. Add more flour as needed to make a soft, rollable dough.
Roll out on a floured surface to 1/3 inch thick. Cut with a round cutter (or drop by spoonfuls and flatten).
Place well apart on the greased baking sheet.
Bake about 10 minutes until set. Cool on a wire rack.

title: Gooseberry Trifle
original_recipe:
GOOSEBERRY TRIFLE

One quart of gooseberries, sugar to taste, one pint of custard, a
plateful of whipped cream.

Put the gooseberries into a jar, with sufficient moist sugar to
sweeten them, and boil them until reduced to a pulp. Put this pulp at
the bottom of a trifle dish; pour over it a pint of custard, and, when
cold, cover with whipped cream. The cream should be whipped the day
before it is wanted for table, as it will then be so much firmer and
more solid. This dish may be garnished as fancy dictates.

title: Hard Sauce
original_recipe:
NO. 39.--HARD SAUCE.

    4 tablespoons soft butter
    3/4 cup powdered sugar
    1/2 teaspoon vanilla
    beaten white of 1 egg

      1. Make bowl and spoon hot with boiling water.

      2. "Cream" or rub butter and sugar together, adding
      sugar by spoonfuls.

      3. Add vanilla.

      4. Beat in the white of egg.

      5. Put in a cool place until needed.
modern_instructions: Warm the mixing bowl and spoon briefly with hot water, then dry.
Beat the soft butter until fluffy.
Add powdered sugar a spoonful at a time, beating well after each addition, until the mixture is light and creamy.
Add vanilla and mix.
Beat the egg white to soft peaks in a separate bowl. Fold into the butter-sugar mixture.
Transfer to a small dish and refrigerate until needed. The sauce will firm up when chilled.

title: Jacket-Boiled Potatoes
original_recipe:
NO. 4.--JACKET-BOILED POTATOES.

      1. Scrub rather small potatoes well.

      2. Pare a ring around each the long way; drop into
      cold water.

      3. Drain; cover with boiling water; add 1 tablespoon
      salt.

      4. Let boil about 35 minutes, or until a fork will
      easily pierce the largest.

      5. Drain off all the water, and set pan at back of
      stove to dry off the potatoes.

      6. Serve in their jackets.
modern_ingredients: 8–12 small potatoes (red or Yukon Gold work well)
modern_instructions: Scrub potatoes well under running water.
Using a vegetable peeler or paring knife, peel a 1/2-inch wide ring around the middle of each potato (around the equator), leaving the rest of the skin on. Drop into cold water as you work.
Drain the potatoes. Place in a pot and cover with boiling water. Add 1 tablespoon salt.
Bring to a boil, then reduce to a steady boil and cook about 35 minutes, or until a fork pierces the largest potato easily.
Drain completely. Return pot to low heat for a minute to dry out the potatoes, or leave uncovered in a warm spot.
Serve in their skins.

      5. Throw the heated milk on potatoes.

      6. Beat with a wire fork until creamy.

      7. Pile lightly on a hot dish. Serve uncovered.

      NOTE.--Remember that all measurements are level or
      even with the top. To divide a spoonful, cut it
      through the middle lengthwise, for a half; and across
      that, for one-quarter of a spoonful.
modern_ingredients: 4 medium potatoes, peeled and boiled (see No. 6)
modern_instructions: Boil and drain potatoes following No. 6. Shake dry over low heat.
Mash the potatoes in the pot while still very hot.
Add butter, salt, and pepper.
Pour in the warmed milk.
Beat vigorously with a fork, hand mixer, or potato masher until creamy and fluffy — do not overbeat or they will become gluey.
Pile lightly onto a warmed serving dish and serve immediately, uncovered.

title: Milk Toast
original_recipe:
NO. 3.--MILK TOAST.

    1 tablespoon butter
    1 tablespoon flour
    1 cup milk
    1/4 teaspoon salt
    3 slices toast

      1. Make ready the toast.

      2. Heat the milk until smoking hot.

      3. Melt the butter in a small saucepan.

      4. Throw the flour into the butter. Cook until it
      bubbles a little, stirring all the time. Take from the
      fire.

      5. Pour 1/3 the milk upon the butter and flour, a
      little at a time, stirring with the back of a spoon to
      press out the lumps.

      6. Place over fire, and gradually stir in the
      remaining milk.

      7. Add the salt. Let boil a minute.

      8. Put slices of toast in a heated dish; pour the
      sauce over and serve hot.

      All measures are made level with the top of cup or
      spoon. To measure level spoonful:

      Fill spoon heaping full, and level it off with the
      back of a knife.

      For half spoonful, cut through lengthwise.

      For quarter spoonful, divide a half spoonful across.

      A saltspoon is 1/8 teaspoon.
modern_instructions: Make 3 slices of toast and keep warm.
Heat the milk in a small saucepan over medium heat until steaming but not boiling. Remove from heat.
In a separate small saucepan, melt the butter over medium heat.
Add the flour to the butter and stir constantly until it bubbles slightly, about 1 minute. Remove from heat.
Slowly pour about 1/3 of the hot milk into the butter-flour mixture, stirring vigorously with the back of a spoon to work out any lumps.
Return to medium heat and gradually stir in the remaining milk.
Add the salt. Let the sauce boil gently for 1 minute, stirring constantly, until thickened.
Arrange toast in a warm, shallow dish. Pour the hot sauce over the toast and serve immediately.

title: Omelet
original_recipe:
NO. 13.--OMELET.

      Two eggs to each person.

      1. Separate yolks and whites, putting them into
      different bowls.

      2. Add dash of salt to whites, and dash of salt and
      white pepper to yolks.

      3. Add cold water to whites, allowing 1 teaspoon to
      each.

      4. Add cold water to yolks, allowing 1 tablespoon to
      each.

      5. Beat both very light.

      6. Melt 1 tablespoon butter in a smooth frying pan.

      7. Pour in yolks. Let cook a moment.

      8. Spread whites over yolks, making a little hole in
      the center for steam to escape.

      9. Cook slowly for 5 minutes, or until the puffed up
      whites look dry.

      10. Fold one half over the other.

      11. Turn out on a warm platter.

      12. Trim with parsley and serve at once.
modern_instructions: Separate yolks and whites into two bowls.
Add a dash of salt and 1 tablespoon cold water to the yolks; beat until light and slightly thickened.
Add a dash of salt and 1 teaspoon cold water to the whites; beat until stiff peaks form.
Melt butter in a smooth 8-inch frying pan over medium-low heat.
Pour in the beaten yolks and let cook undisturbed for 1 minute.
Spoon the beaten whites over the yolks, spreading evenly. Make a small hole in the center for steam to escape.
Cover loosely and cook slowly for 4–5 minutes until the whites look dry and set.
Fold one half over the other with a spatula.
Slide onto a warmed platter. Garnish with fresh parsley and serve at once.

title: Pan-Broiled Beefsteak
original_recipe:
NO. 40.--PAN-BROILED BEEFSTEAK.

      1. Heat an empty frying pan to blue heat, or until it
      smokes. _No greasing is necessary._

      2. Put in the steak.

      3. Cook half minute.

      4. Turn on other side.

      5. Cook about 4 minutes.

      6. Turn, and cook about 5 minutes longer.

      7. Place on a hot platter, and spread with butter.

      8. Sprinkle with a little pepper and salt, and 1
      tablespoon finely chopped parsley.

      9. Squeeze over it a little lemon juice.

      10. Cover with another platter.

      11. Remove top platter just before serving.

      NOTE.--Mutton or Lamb Chops are pan-broiled in the
      same way.
modern_ingredients: 1 beefsteak (sirloin, ribeye, or NY strip, about 3/4 to 1 inch thick)
Salt and black pepper to taste
modern_instructions: Pat the steak dry with paper towels (moisture prevents proper searing).
Heat a heavy cast-iron or stainless steel skillet over high heat until it begins to smoke — 'blue heat' in the original recipe.
Add the steak to the dry, ungreased pan.
Cook undisturbed for 30 seconds.
Flip and cook 3–4 minutes.
Flip again and cook 4–5 minutes more (total 8–10 minutes for medium — adjust for your thickness and preference).
Transfer to a warm platter. Spread butter over the top while it rests.
Sprinkle with salt, pepper, and chopped parsley.
Squeeze lemon juice over the top.
Cover with an inverted platter for 2 minutes. Remove the top platter at the table before serving.

title: Pastry
original_recipe:
NO. 34.--PASTRY.

    1 cup flour
    1/4 teaspoon salt
    1/3 cup lard
    1/3 cup cold water

      (Use as little water as possible.)

      1. Sift salt and flour into a bowl.

      2. Rub lard into flour with finger tips until like
      coarse powder.

      3. Add half the cold water, stirring with a knife to
      form a stiff ball. Keep this on one side of the bowl.

      4. Stir in more water until the remaining flour forms
      a ball. Press these balls together.

      5. Roll out. Fit to pie plate. Trim off overhanging
      edges.

      6. Bake in a hot oven.
modern_ingredients: 1/3 cup cold lard (or cold vegetable shortening; cold butter also works)
modern_instructions: Sift flour and salt into a bowl.
Add cold lard in small pieces. Rub into the flour with fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse, uneven crumbs — some pieces of fat should still be visible.
Add cold water a tablespoon at a time, stirring with a knife after each addition. Use only enough water to bring the dough together into a ball — err on the side of less water.
Press the dough into a flat disc, wrap in plastic wrap, and refrigerate for at least 15 minutes.
Roll out on a lightly floured surface to 1/8 inch thickness.
Fit into a pie plate or tart pans. Trim edges and crimp.
For a blind-baked shell: prick with a fork, line with parchment and weights, and bake at 425°F (220°C) for 12–15 minutes.

title: Peppermint Drops

PEPPERMINT DROPS

One cupful of sugar crushed fine, and just moistened with boiling
water, then boiled five minutes; then take from the fire and add cream
of tartar the size of a pea; mix well and add four or five drops of
oil of peppermint. Beat briskly until the mixture whitens, then drop
quickly upon white paper. Have the cream of tartar and oil of
peppermint measured while the sugar is boiling. If it sugars before it
is all dropped, add a little water and boil a minute or two.

title: Pickaninny Fudge
original_recipe:
NO. 29.--PICKANINNY FUDGE.

    2 cups sugar
    1 cup milk
    2 tablespoons butter
    4 squares chocolate (4 ounces)
    5 drops vanilla

      1. Put sugar, milk, chocolate and butter together in a
      pan.

      2. Boil until a few drops harden when dropped into
      cold water.

      3. Butter a platter.

      4. Add Vanilla to candy and stir while it cools.

      5. Cut into squares with a buttered knife while still
      soft.
modern_ingredients: 4 ounces (4 squares) unsweetened baking chocolate, broken up
modern_instructions: Butter a platter or an 8x8-inch baking pan and set aside.
Combine sugar, milk, butter, and chocolate in a heavy-bottomed saucepan.
Cook over medium heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves and the chocolate melts.

Boil, stirring occasionally, until the mixture reaches 234–238°F (soft-ball stage) on a candy thermometer, or until a small amount dropped into cold water forms a soft ball you can flatten with your fingers.
Remove from heat. Add vanilla but do not stir yet — let cool undisturbed for 5 minutes.
Beat vigorously with a wooden spoon or hand mixer until the fudge thickens and loses its gloss.
Quickly pour onto the buttered platter or pan.
While still slightly warm and soft, cut into squares with a buttered knife.

title: Poached Eggs
original_recipe:
NO. 23.--POACHED EGGS.

      1. Put a pint (2 cups) boiling water into a shallow
      pan. Add 1/2 teaspoon salt.

      2. Break egg in a saucer.

      3. Whirl the water 'round and 'round with a spoon, and
      draw pan back on stove where it will simmer, but not
      boil hard.

      4. Slide the egg from the saucer into the water.

      5. Let stand where the water will not boil hard.

      6. Cook until the white is set and the yolk filmed
      over.

      7. Lift out with a skimmer. Drain on a cloth and
      serve on toast.
modern_ingredients: Optional: 1 tablespoon white vinegar (helps whites set)
modern_instructions: Fill a shallow pan with 2 cups of water. Add salt (and vinegar if using). Bring to a gentle simmer.
Crack each egg into a small ramekin or saucer.
Gently swirl the simmering water with a spoon to create a gentle spiral.
Slide one egg at a time from the saucer into the center of the swirling water.
Reduce heat so the water barely simmers — it should not boil hard.
Cook 3–4 minutes until the whites are completely set and the yolks are still soft.
Lift out with a slotted spoon and drain on a clean cloth or paper towel.
Serve immediately on buttered toast.

title: Potato Soup
original_recipe:
NO. 8.--POTATO SOUP.

      Place on the table:

    3 freshly boiled potatoes
    1 onion
    butter
    parsley
    flour dredger
    pepper
    salt
    1 pint milk (2 cups)

      1. Put the milk in the upper part of a double boiler,
      half filling the under part with boiling water.

      2. Throw in 2 slices of onion, and put double boiler
      on the stove for 10 minutes.

      3. Mash potatoes and add to the hot milk.

      4. Add 1 teaspoon salt and a dash of pepper.

      5. Put through a wire strainer, rubbing the potatoes
      through with a spoon.

      6. Put into double boiler and place on stove.

      7. Melt 1 tablespoon butter in a little pan.

      8. Throw into it 1/2 tablespoon flour. Stir well.

      9. Dip a little of the hot milk on this, stirring
      well, then pour into the soup.

      10. Let boil 10 minutes.

      11. Add 1 tablespoon chopped parsley. If too thick,
      add hot water or milk.

      12. Serve very hot.
modern_instructions: Heat the milk in a double boiler or in a saucepan set over a pot of simmering water. Add the onion slices and heat for 10 minutes.
Remove onion slices. Mash the boiled potatoes and stir them into the hot milk.
Add salt and pepper.
Press the mixture through a wire mesh strainer (or use a blender for a smoother result), then return to the double boiler.
In a small pan, melt butter over medium heat. Add flour and stir for 1 minute.
Ladle a little of the hot soup into the flour mixture, stir smooth, then pour back into the soup pot.
Cook for 10 minutes over gentle heat. If too thick, add hot water or milk.
Stir in fresh parsley and serve piping hot.

title: Queen of Hearts Tarts

      Spread cooked heart-shaped pastry shells with
      preserved cherries. See No. 34.

modern_instructions: Make Pastry dough (No. 34) and chill 15 minutes.

title: Rice Pudding
original_recipe:
NO. 24.--RICE PUDDING.

    3 tablespoons rice
    1/4 cup sugar
    1/8 teaspoon salt
    sprinkling nutmeg
    4 cups milk, or 3 cups milk and 1 cup water

      1. Pick over the rice, and wash by putting in a
      strainer and shaking in a pan of water.

      2. Butter the baking dish.

      3. Stir rice, sugar and salt into the milk.

      4. Pour into the baking dish.

      5. Sprinkle with nutmeg.

      6. Cover with a lid, and bake slowly 2 hours.

      7. Uncover and brown 1/2 hour.

      NOTE.--Seeded raisins may be added before browning. If
      desired, a thin narrow shaving of the outside rind of
      a lemon may be used as flavoring. Put this in the milk
      with the rice.
modern_ingredients: 3 tablespoons short-grain or long-grain white rice
4 cups whole milk (or 3 cups milk + 1 cup water)
Optional: 1/4 cup raisins, strip of lemon zest
modern_instructions: Preheat oven to 325°F (165°C). Butter a 1.5-quart baking dish.
Rinse rice in a strainer and shake dry.
Combine rice, sugar, salt, and milk in the baking dish and stir.
Sprinkle the surface generously with freshly grated nutmeg.
If using lemon zest, add one strip to the milk.
Cover with a lid or foil and bake for 2 hours, stirring once or twice during the first hour.
Remove lid/foil. If adding raisins, stir them in now.
Bake uncovered for 30 more minutes until the top is golden brown.
Serve warm or cold.

title: Salad Dressing
original_recipe:
NO. 35.--SALAD DRESSING.

    1 teaspoon salt
    3/4 teaspoon mustard
    1-1/2 tablespoons sugar
    2 teaspoons flour
    3/4 cup sour cream (or milk)
    1 egg
    1 tablespoon vinegar
    1 teaspoon butter
    A few grains of red pepper.

      1. Beat egg.

      2. Mix mustard, sugar, flour, salt and pepper.

      3. Add egg slowly, beating well at the same time.

      4. Add milk. Beat.

      5. Cook in a sauce pan placed in boiling water.

      6. Stir until it thickens like cream. Remove from heat
      at once.

      7. Add vinegar very slowly, beating all the while.

      8. Stir in the butter.

      Serve cold on lettuce or sliced tomatoes.
modern_ingredients: A few grains of cayenne pepper
modern_instructions: In a bowl, mix mustard, sugar, flour, salt, and cayenne.
Add the beaten egg slowly, whisking well.
Stir in the sour cream (or milk). Whisk until smooth.

title: Sauce or Gravy for Boiled Mutton
original_recipe:
NO. 26.--SAUCE OR GRAVY FOR BOILED MUTTON.

      1. After cooling and skimming off the fat, measure the
      water in which the meat was boiled.

      2. To each cupful, allow

      1 tablespoon flour, 1/4 teaspoon salt, 1/2 teaspoon
      vinegar.

      3. Moisten these with a little cold water.

      4. Stir them into the boiling gravy.

      5. Add 1 tablespoon finely minced parsley.
modern_ingredients: 2 cups cooking liquid from Boiled Mutton (No. 25), defatted
Small amount of cold water to make a paste
modern_instructions: Cool and degrease the cooking liquid from No. 25 (either by skimming or refrigerating and removing the solidified fat).
Bring the defatted cooking liquid to a boil in a saucepan.
In a small bowl, mix flour, salt, and vinegar with just enough cold water to make a thin paste.
Whisk the flour paste into the boiling gravy gradually, stirring constantly.
Boil gently for 2–3 minutes until the gravy thickens slightly.
Stir in minced parsley. Taste for seasoning and serve hot over the meat.

title: Soft Custard
original_recipe:
NO. 20.--SOFT CUSTARD.

    1 cup milk
    1 egg
    2 tablespoons sugar
    dash of salt
    nutmeg

      1. Heat the milk smoking hot.

      2. Beat egg. Add sugar and salt.

      3. Pour the hot milk on, beating well.

      4. Pour into upper part of double boiler, or set pan
      in boiling water.

      5. Cook until it thickens, or until a coating will be
      formed on a clean spoon when dipped into the custard.

      6. Sprinkle with a few gratings of nutmeg.

      7. Serve cold.
modern_instructions: Heat milk in a small saucepan over medium heat until steaming (just below a boil). Remove from heat.
In a bowl, beat the egg with sugar and salt until slightly pale.
Slowly pour the hot milk into the egg mixture, beating constantly to prevent curdling.
Pour the mixture into the top of a double boiler (or set bowl over a pot of simmering water).
Cook over medium-low heat, stirring constantly, until the custard thickens enough to coat a clean spoon — about 8–12 minutes. Do not let it boil.
Remove from heat immediately. Strain through a fine sieve if desired.
Sprinkle lightly with grated nutmeg.
Refrigerate and serve cold, or serve warm over rice or fruit.

title: Sponge Cake
original_recipe:
NO. 31.--SPONGE CAKE.

    2 eggs
    1 cup sugar
    1/3 cup boiling water
    1/2 teaspoon vanilla (or lemon extract)
    1 cup flour
    1-1/2 teaspoon baking powder
    dash of salt

      1. Separate yolks of eggs and beat in a large bowl.

      2. Add gradually half the sugar, stirring well.

      3. Add the boiling water slowly.

      4. Add remaining sugar, and the flavoring.

      5. Sift together twice flour, baking powder and salt.

      6. Sift by small quantities into the mixture, beating
      well each time.

      7. Beat whites of eggs and fold them into the cake.

      8. Grease pan. Toss around in it 1/4 cup flour. Throw
      out. This prevents sticking.

      9. Pour in the cake.

      10. Bake in a moderate oven about 35 minutes.
modern_instructions: Preheat oven to 350°F (175°C). Grease a 9-inch round cake pan, dust with flour, and shake out the excess.
Beat egg yolks in a large bowl. Gradually add half the sugar, beating until thick and pale.
Slowly add the boiling water, beating constantly.
Add remaining sugar and vanilla. Beat well.
Sift together flour, baking powder, and salt twice. Add to the egg mixture in small batches, beating well after each addition.
In a separate bowl, beat egg whites to stiff peaks. Gently fold into the batter.
Pour batter into prepared pan.
Bake 30–35 minutes until the cake shrinks slightly from the sides and a toothpick inserted in the center comes out clean.
Cool in pan 10 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack.

title: Steamed Rice
original_recipe:
NO. 21.--STEAMED RICE.

    1/2 cup rice
    2 cups boiling water
    1/2 teaspoon salt

      1. Wash rice. This may be easily done by putting in a
      strainer and shaking in a pan of cold water.

      2. Put with the salt and boiling water, into the upper
      part of a double boiler.

      3. Pour boiling water into lower part, and cook 1
      hour.

      4. To test whether it is done, press several grains
      between the fingers. If not perfectly soft, cook
      longer.

      5. If it becomes hard and dry, add a little boiling
      water from time to time.

      6. A few raisins are sometimes cooked with the rice.
      Add these during the last fifteen minutes of cooking
      to prevent discoloring.

      7. Serve with soft custard or cream and sugar. Plain
      cooked rice may be served as a vegetable.
modern_ingredients: Optional: 1/4 cup raisins (added in last 15 minutes)
modern_instructions: Rinse rice in a strainer under cold running water until the water runs clear.
Combine rice, boiling water, and salt in the top of a double boiler.
Fill the bottom of the double boiler with boiling water and place over medium heat.
Cover and steam for 45–60 minutes, checking occasionally and adding boiling water to the bottom pot as needed.
Test for doneness by pressing a few grains between fingers — they should be completely soft throughout.
If adding raisins, stir them in during the last 15 minutes.
Serve as a side vegetable, or top with soft custard, cream, and sugar as a dessert.

title: Stuffed Eggs
original_recipe:
NO. 28.--STUFFED EGGS.

      1. Hard-boil eggs.

      2. Drop into cold water. Remove shells.

      3. Cut each in half lengthwise.

      4. Turn out yolks into a bowl.

      5. Carefully place whites together in pairs.

      6. Mash yolks with back of a spoon.

      7. For every 6 yolks, put into the bowl

    1 tablespoon olive oil or melted butter
    1/2 teaspoon mustard (the kind prepared for table)
    1/2 teaspoon salt
    dash cayenne pepper

      8. Rub these together thoroughly with the yolks.

      9. Make little balls of this paste, the size of the
      yolks.

      10. Fit one ball into each pair whites.

      NOTE.--If used for table, serve with White Sauce
      poured around them. If used for picnic, wrap waxed
      paper around each until needed.
modern_ingredients: 1 tablespoon olive oil or melted butter
modern_instructions: Hard-boil eggs (No. 10). Cool in cold water, then peel.
Cut each egg in half lengthwise. Remove yolks into a bowl; set whites aside in matching pairs.
Mash yolks thoroughly with the back of a spoon until smooth.
Add olive oil (or butter), mustard, salt, and cayenne. Mix until completely smooth.
Roll the yolk mixture into small balls roughly the size of the original yolks.
Fit one ball into each egg white half.
For a party: serve on a platter with White Sauce (No. 14) poured around them, or garnish with a dash of paprika.
For a picnic: wrap each stuffed egg in wax paper to keep until serving.

title: Tea
original_recipe:
NO. 12.--TEA.

      1. Half fill the teapot with boiling water. Let stand
      until thoroughly hot. Pour out.

      2. Put into it 1 teaspoon tea for each cup needed.

      3. Pour in freshly boiling water, allowing 1 cup to
      every teaspoon tea.

      4. Let stand for 5 minutes in a very warm place, but
      do not let it boil. Stir, and serve.

      If not used immediately, strain into another heated
      pot.
modern_ingredients: 4 teaspoons loose-leaf black tea (or 4 tea bags)
4 cups freshly boiling water, plus extra to warm the pot
modern_instructions: Pour boiling water into the teapot to warm it. Let stand 1 minute, then discard the water.
Add 1 teaspoon loose-leaf tea per cup to the warmed pot.
Pour freshly boiling water over the tea — 1 cup per teaspoon of tea.
Cover and let steep 4–5 minutes in a warm spot. Do not let it boil.
Stir, then strain into cups or a warmed second pot if not serving immediately.

title: Thimble Biscuits
original_recipe:
NO. 16.--THIMBLE BISCUITS.

      (Nice for Dolls' Tea Party.)

    1 cup flour
    2 teaspoons baking powder
    1 tablespoon butter
    1/2 cup milk (scant)
    1/4 teaspoon salt

      1. A hot oven and a greased pan.

      2. Sift flour, baking powder and salt three times.

      3. Rub butter lightly into the flour.

      4. Pour the milk on gradually, mixing all the while
      with a knife, until a soft dough is formed.

      (Perhaps not all the milk will be needed.)

      5. Turn the dough on a well-floured board.

      6. Pat it with the rolling pin until 1/4 inch thick.

      7. Use a large thimble as a cutter, and cut biscuits
      as close to each other as possible.

      8. Place in pan a little distance apart.

      9. Bake about 5 minutes.
modern_ingredients: Scant 1/2 cup whole milk (a little less than 1/2 cup)
modern_instructions: Preheat oven to 450°F (230°C). Grease a baking sheet.
Sift together flour, baking powder, and salt three times.
Cut in the cold butter using fingertips or a pastry cutter until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs.
Add milk gradually, mixing gently with a knife or fork just until a soft dough forms. Use only as much milk as needed.
Turn dough onto a well-floured surface.
Pat or roll to 1/4 inch thickness.
Cut out rounds using a large thimble, a 1-inch round cutter, or the mouth of a small bottle. Cut pieces close together to minimize waste.
Place on prepared pan a little apart.
Bake 4–6 minutes until just golden.

title: Walnut Kisses
original_recipe:
NO. 30.--WALNUT KISSES.

    1 cup brown sugar
    1/2 cup granulated sugar
    1/4 cup cold water
    white 1 egg

      1. Boil sugars and water together until a few drops
      harden when dropped into cold water.

      2. Beat white of egg stiff.

      3. Pour the sugar syrup very slowly upon the white of
      egg, beating all the time.

      4. Butter a platter.

      5. Drop by tablespoonfuls on the platter.

      6. Put a half walnut on each little mound or "kiss."
modern_instructions: Butter a baking sheet or piece of parchment paper and set aside.

Combine both sugars and the water in a small saucepan. Cook over medium heat, stirring until dissolved, then boil without stirring until the mixture reaches 240°F (soft-ball stage), or until a few drops dropped into cold water form a firm but pliable ball.
While the sugar is cooking, beat the egg white to stiff peaks in a large bowl.
When the sugar syrup is ready, pour it very slowly in a thin stream onto the beaten egg white, beating constantly with a hand mixer at high speed.
Continue beating until the mixture holds stiff, glossy peaks and is slightly cooled.
Quickly drop by tablespoonfuls onto the prepared surface.
Press a walnut half firmly into the center of each mound.
Let stand until set, about 30 minutes.

title: White Sauce
original_recipe:
NO. 14.--WHITE SAUCE.

    2 tablespoons butter
    2 tablespoons flour
    1 cup hot milk
    1/4 teaspoon salt
    dash of pepper

      1. Melt butter in a sauce pan.

      2. Mix pepper and salt with flour.

      3. Throw into the butter, stirring thoroughly. Cook
      until it bubbles a little.

      4. Pour 1/3 of the milk very slowly on this, stirring
      and beating well.

      5. Place over fire and stir in the rest of the milk a
      little at a time.

      6. Let boil a minute.
modern_ingredients: Dash of white or black pepper
modern_instructions: Melt butter in a small saucepan over medium heat.
Mix salt and pepper with the flour. Add to the melted butter all at once and stir constantly for 1–2 minutes until bubbling (this is the roux).
Remove from heat. Pour about 1/3 of the warm milk in very slowly, stirring vigorously to prevent lumps.
Return to medium heat and stir in the remaining milk a little at a time.
Continue stirring until the sauce reaches a boil and thickens, about 1 minute.
Use immediately over vegetables, toast, or as a base for other dishes.
